filer: Pennsylvania Office of Consumer AdvocateDelaware Division of the Public Advocate
source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5242__Pennsylvania-Office-of-Consumer-AdvocateDelaware-D/Joint Consumer Advocates (PA, DE) Comments - Large Loads ANOPR RM26-4.txt
overall_summary: The Pennsylvania OCA and Delaware DPA file jointly to protect residential ratepayers from cost-shifting and reliability degradation caused by large load interconnection. They support FERC jurisdiction over transmission and wholesale market aspects of large load interconnection but insist the Commission must not preempt existing state authority over local distribution and end-use customer interconnection. They call for 100% cost responsibility for large loads with no crediting mechanism, a standardized queue with deposits and withdrawal penalties, adherence to all NERC reliability guidelines, and a package of ratepayer protections including minimum demand charges, minimum contract terms, and financial collateral requirements.

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates seek to ensure that these ratepayers do not pay higher rates for less reliable (i.e., inferior) service resulting from the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their load through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.
quotes.concern: Residential ratepayers should not bear costs or reliability impacts from large load interconnections that lack offsetting capacity

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support jurisdictional federal avenues for regulating the interstate transmission and wholesale market aspects of large load interconnection, while respecting states' existing authority, including the authority to interconnect large loads at the distribution level.
quotes.concern: FERC jurisdiction is appropriate for transmission and wholesale markets but not for distribution-level interconnections

quotes.text: FERC has never claimed the kind of jurisdiction over end-use customer interconnection that the ANOPR proposes.
quotes.concern: The ANOPR's assertion of jurisdiction over end-use customer interconnection is unprecedented and legally risky

quotes.text: The ANOPR risks lengthy, burdensome, and meritorious legal challenges to the extent that it ignores the FPA's and FERC's explicit acknowledgements of state jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: An overbroad NOPR could face successful legal challenge from states defending their reserved jurisdiction

quotes.text: A FERC final rule that includes federal preemption of any existing, traditional state authority will face an uphill battle under the U.S. Supreme Court's new standard of review for agency statutory interpretation.
quotes.concern: Post-Loper Bright, courts will not defer to FERC on ambiguous jurisdictional claims and could invalidate a preemptive rule

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support the ANOPR's minimum threshold of 20 megawatts (MW) for both large loads and hybrid facilities, and this threshold should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement rather than to a single point of interconnection.
quotes.concern: The 20 MW threshold should apply to aggregate demand to prevent load-splitting to evade requirements

quotes.text: The interconnection study process should not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.
quotes.concern: Study timelines must preserve reliability and full identification of network upgrade costs

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support establishing a large load and hybrid interconnection queue that is centrally managed by the same entity that manages/administers resource adequacy processes, like PJM. This queue should have standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers and RTOs with useful information to forecast demand on their systems accurately.
quotes.concern: A centrally managed queue with standardized financial commitments is needed to deter speculative large load projects

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates agree that load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism. Such a mechanism risks other ratepayers paying for the network upgrades that are the responsibility of the load and hybrid facilities.
quotes.concern: A crediting mechanism would shift upgrade costs to other ratepayers and violates cost causation

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates fully support revisiting a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customer, as opposed to a general benefits test, because but for the large load, the transmission owner would not have incurred the costs to upgrade the network facilities to serve that customer and the upgrades would not have been put into service without the interconnection of the large load addition.
quotes.concern: A but-for causation standard should govern cost allocation so large loads pay for all upgrades they necessitate

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support large loads and hybrid facilities that interconnect to the system only if they bring sufficient capacity (either through generation and reserves or capacity-backed demand response) to offset the additional load brought onto the grid.
quotes.concern: Large loads should be conditioned on providing or procuring enough capacity to offset their grid impact

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support a package of ratepayer protections to the extent that the Commission finds them to be jurisdictional: 1) minimum contractual duration, renewal, and cancellation provisions; 2) minimum demand payments and demand ramping requirements; 3) financial collateral and guarantees.
quotes.concern: A layered set of contractual and financial protections is needed to prevent stranded cost risk to other ratepayers

quotes.text: The Joint Consumer Advocates support the establishment of clear contract terms regarding duration, renewal, and termination fees. Additionally, a minimum demand charge of 85% of forecasted peak demand at the conclusion of the ramp period would be reasonable.
quotes.concern: An 85% minimum demand charge protects against stranded transmission costs when large loads reduce or leave

quotes.text: The interconnection study process should comply with all NERC guidelines and Reliability Standards and not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.
quotes.concern: Full NERC compliance must be maintained; expedited timelines cannot override reliability standards

bins.name: Preserve State Interconnection Authority
bins.description: The filers support FERC jurisdiction over interstate transmission and wholesale markets but strongly oppose any preemption of state authority over end-use customer interconnection and local distribution facilities. They cite Order 888, the FPA's explicit reservations, Loper Bright, and the Major Questions Doctrine as reasons a broad jurisdictional assertion would face serious legal risk.

bins.name: 20 MW Aggregate Threshold
bins.description: The filers endorse the ANOPR's 20 MW threshold and specify it should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement across all points of interconnection to prevent splitting loads to avoid coverage.

bins.name: No Shortcuts on Study Quality
bins.description: The filers support expedited study processes for curtailable and dispatchable large loads but insist studies must fully comply with NERC guidelines and reliability standards. They oppose mandatory timelines for EDC studies given the complexity of large load types.

bins.name: Standardized Queue With Deposits
bins.description: The filers support a centrally managed interconnection queue with standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and improve demand forecasting accuracy.

bins.name: Full Cost Responsibility, No Credits
bins.description: The filers endorse 100% cost responsibility for network upgrades assigned to large loads with no crediting mechanism, and advocate for a but-for causation test to ensure all upgrade costs driven by the large load are directly assigned rather than rolled into network rates.

bins.name: Ratepayer Protections Package
bins.description: The filers urge FERC to require minimum contract terms, an 85% minimum demand charge, demand ramping requirements, and financial collateral from large loads so that stranded transmission costs do not fall on other ratepayers if a large load reduces load or exits.

bins.name: Comprehensive, NERC-Compliant Studies
bins.description: The filers support studying large loads and hybrid facilities together with generation to minimize upgrade costs, but insist the process be technically sound, comply with all NERC guidelines, and not sacrifice reliability or accuracy for speed.

bins.name: Cost Causation for All Grid Costs
bins.description: The filers call for strict cost causation throughout the rulemaking: large loads must pay 100% of upgrade costs without credits, face an 85% minimum demand charge, and provide financial collateral so no costs are shifted to residential ratepayers.

bins.name: Capacity Offset Required for Co-location
bins.description: The filers support co-located and hybrid facilities but condition interconnection approval on the large load bringing sufficient offsetting capacity to avoid worsening resource adequacy in the PJM region.

bins.name: PJM Resource Adequacy Crisis Context
bins.description: The filers situate their comments in PJM's current resource adequacy and affordability crisis, where 30 GW of projected peak load growth through 2030 comes from data centers and capacity prices have risen more than tenfold, underscoring the urgency of protective requirements.

bins.name: Protect Residential Ratepayers
bins.description: The overarching theme of the filing is protecting residential electricity consumers in Pennsylvania and Delaware from paying higher rates or receiving less reliable service because of large load interconnection. The filers propose a multi-layer protection package covering cost assignment, minimum charges, contract terms, and financial collateral.

bins.name: Jurisdictional Overreach Invites Litigation
bins.description: The filers warn that an overbroad NOPR asserting federal jurisdiction over end-use customer interconnection will face meritorious legal challenges from states, particularly given Loper Bright's elimination of Chevron deference and the possible applicability of the Major Questions Doctrine.

bins.name: Large Load Capacity Offset Condition
bins.description: The filers support interconnecting large loads and hybrid facilities only when they bring or procure sufficient capacity to offset their addition to grid load, whether through on-site generation, reserves, or capacity-backed demand response.

bins.name: Full NERC Standard Compliance
bins.description: The filers support requiring all utilities and PJM to abide by NERC Reliability Standards and guidelines for large load interconnection, and ask that any deviations be disclosed to stakeholders with an explanation.
